questions.q: What cache pattern reads from the source of truth on a miss and then stores the result?
What eviction policy removes the least recently used item?
What does TTL stand for in caching?
What do you call a key that gets disproportionate traffic?
What metric is hits divided by total requests?

questions.a: Caching a miss or empty result to avoid repeated lookups.
Updates can race with reads, so stale copies can survive until eviction or TTL.

questions.q: Why does a cache stampede happen?
questions.a: Many requests miss the same key at once and all try to rebuild it.

questions.q: Why would you choose write-through over cache-aside?
questions.a: You want simpler consistency and can afford slower writes.
Data can be lost if the cache fails before dirty entries are flushed.

questions.q: Why can LFU beat LRU on skewed workloads?
questions.a: Frequency matters more than recency when a few keys dominate traffic.

questions.q: What does a Bloom filter help with in caching?
questions.a: It helps prevent cache penetration by cheaply rejecting known misses.

questions.q: When would you place a CDN in front of an app cache?
questions.a: When you serve static or globally read-heavy content and want edge latency reduction.

questions.q: When is cache-aside a good fit for session data?
questions.a: When the source of truth is durable elsewhere and session reads need low latency.

questions.q: How would you reduce stampede risk on a hot key?
questions.a: Use request coalescing, stale-while-revalidate, jittered TTLs, or a per-key lock.

questions.q: When is write-through better than cache-aside?
questions.a: When you want a single write path and can tolerate the extra write latency.

questions.q: What is the tradeoff of TTL-based invalidation?
questions.a: It is simple, but you accept a bounded window of stale reads.

questions.q: When would you skip caching entirely?
questions.a: When the hit rate is low, the data is cheap to fetch, or freshness is critical.
